# 2026-03-21 11:57:23Z

<!-- cursoride Session task-fd8ba68c-38ed-407c-a8cb-80d753618829 (2026-03-21 11:57:23Z) -->

_**User**_

You are on the TestPyPI Trusted Publisher Management page. The user is logged in. You need to add 10 pending publishers.

CRITICAL: The submit pattern that works is:
1. Use browser_fill_form to fill all 5 fields at once
2. Click the Add button 
3. Press Enter key (this is what actually submits the form)
4. Take a snapshot to confirm the form was cleared (confirms submission)

The form field refs after each page refresh are consistently:
- e20: TestPyPI Project Name
- e21: Owner  
- e22: Repository name
- e23: Workflow name
- e24: Environment name
- e26: Add button

For ALL packages, use these constant values:
- Owner: firstunicorn
- Repository: python-web-toolkit
- Workflow: publish-testpypi.yml
- Environment: testpypi

The 10 packages to add (in order):
1. python-cqrs-dispatcher
2. python-domain-events
3. python-dto-mappers
4. python-infrastructure-exceptions
5. python-input-validation
6. python-mediator
7. python-outbox-core
8. python-structlog-config
9. sqlalchemy-async-repositories
10. sqlalchemy-async-session-factory

For each package:
1. Lock browser
2. Take snapshot first to confirm form refs
3. Use browser_fill_form with the 5 fields
4. Click the Add button (e26)
5. Press Enter key
6. Take snapshot to verify form cleared (confirms submission)
7. Continue to next package

After all 10 are done, unlock the browser and report which packages succeeded and which failed.

---

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet)**_

I'll help you add the 10 pending publishers to TestPyPI. Let me start by taking a snapshot to see the current page state and confirm the form field references.

role: heading\\n      name: Trusted Publisher Management\\n      ref: e88\\n      level: 1\\n      name: OpenID Connect (OIDC) provides a flexible, credential-free mechanism for delegating publishing authority for a PyPI package to a trusted third party service, like GitHub Actions.\\n      ref: e89\\n      name: PyPI users and projects can use trusted publishers to automate their release processes, without needing to use API tokens or passwords.\\n      ref: e90\\n      name: You can read more about trusted publishers and how to use them here .\\n      ref: e91\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Manage publishers\\n      ref: e92\\n      level: 2\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Projects with active publishers\\n      ref: e93\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Pending publishers\\n      ref: e94\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Add a new pending publisher\\n      ref: e95\\n      level: 2\\n      name: \\\"You can use this page to register \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" trusted publishers.\\\"\\n      ref: e96\\n      name: These publishers behave similarly to trusted publishers registered against specific projects, except that they allow users to create the project if it doesn't already exist. Once the project is create\\n      ref: e97\\n      name: \\\"Configuring a \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher for a project name does not reserve that name. Until the project is created, any other user may create it, including via their own \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher.\\\"\\n      ref: e98\\n      name: Read more about GitHub Actions' OpenID Connect support here .\\n      ref: e99\\n      name: TestPyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e100\\n      name: The project that will be created on TestPyPI when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e101\\n      name: Owner (required)\\n      ref: e102\\n      name: The GitHub organization name or GitHub username that owns the repository\\n      ref: e103\\n      name: Repository name (required)\\n      ref: e104\\n      name: The name of the GitHub repository that contains the publishing workflow\\n      ref: e105\\n      name: Workflow name (required)\\n      ref: e106\\n      name: The filename of the publishing workflow. This file should exist in the .github/workflows/ directory in the repository configured above.\\n      ref: e107\\n      name: Environment name (optional)\\n      ref: e108\\n      name: The name of the GitHub Actions environment that the above workflow uses for publishing. - One existing pending publisher: `fastapi-config-patterns` with GitHub

role: heading\\n      name: Trusted Publisher Management\\n      ref: e88\\n      level: 1\\n      name: OpenID Connect (OIDC) provides a flexible, credential-free mechanism for delegating publishing authority for a PyPI package to a trusted third party service, like GitHub Actions.\\n      ref: e89\\n      name: PyPI users and projects can use trusted publishers to automate their release processes, without needing to use API tokens or passwords.\\n      ref: e90\\n      name: You can read more about trusted publishers and how to use them here .\\n      ref: e91\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Manage publishers\\n      ref: e92\\n      level: 2\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Projects with active publishers\\n      ref: e93\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Pending publishers\\n      ref: e94\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Add a new pending publisher\\n      ref: e95\\n      level: 2\\n      name: \\\"You can use this page to register \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" trusted publishers.\\\"\\n      ref: e96\\n      name: These publishers behave similarly to trusted publishers registered against specific projects, except that they allow users to create the project if it doesn't already exist. Once the project is create\\n      ref: e97\\n      name: \\\"Configuring a \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher for a project name does not reserve that name. Until the project is created, any other user may create it, including via their own \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher.\\\"\\n      ref: e98\\n      name: Read more about GitHub Actions' OpenID Connect support here .\\n      ref: e99\\n      name: TestPyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e100\\n      name: The project that will be created on TestPyPI when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e101\\n      name: Owner (required)\\n      ref: e102\\n      name: The GitHub organization name or GitHub username that owns the repository\\n      ref: e103\\n      name: Repository name (required)\\n      ref: e104\\n      name: The name of the GitHub repository that contains the publishing workflow\\n      ref: e105\\n      name: Workflow name (required)\\n      ref: e106\\n      name: The filename of the publishing workflow. This file should exist in the .github/workflows/ directory in the repository configured above.\\n      ref: e107\\n      name: Environment name (optional)\\n      ref: e108\\n      name: The name of the GitHub Actions environment that the above workflow uses for publishing. This should be configured under the repository's settings. role: listitem\\n      name: Your organizations\\n      ref: e85\\n    - role: listitem\\n      name: Account settings\\n      ref: e86\\n    - role: listitem\\n      name: Publishing\\n      ref: e87\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Trusted Publisher Management\\n      ref: e88\\n      level: 1\\n      name: OpenID Connect (OIDC) provides a flexible, credential-free mechanism for delegating publishing authority for a PyPI package to a trusted third party service, like GitHub Actions.\\n      ref: e89\\n      name: PyPI users and projects can use trusted publishers to automate their release processes, without needing to use API tokens or passwords.\\n      ref: e90\\n      name: You can read more about trusted publishers and how to use them here .\\n      ref: e91\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Manage publishers\\n      ref: e92\\n      level: 2\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Projects with active publishers\\n      ref: e93\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Pending publishers\\n      ref: e94\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Add a new pending publisher\\n      ref: e95\\n      level: 2\\n      name: \\\"You can use this page to register \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" trusted publishers.\\\"\\n      ref: e96\\n      name: These publishers behave similarly to trusted publishers registered against specific projects, except that they allow users to create the project if it doesn't already exist. Once the project is create\\n      ref: e97\\n      name: \\\"Configuring a \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher for a project name does not reserve that name. Until the project is created, any other user may create it, including via their own \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher.\\\"\\n      ref: e98\\n      name: Read more about GitHub Actions' OpenID Connect support here .\\n      ref: e99\\n      name: TestPyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e100\\n      name: The project that will be created on TestPyPI when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e101\\n      name: Owner (required)\\n      ref: e102\\n      name: The GitHub organization name or GitHub username that owns the repository\\n      ref: e103\\n      name: Repository name (required)\\n      ref: e104\\n      name: The name of the GitHub repository that contains the publishing workflow\\n      ref: e105\\n      name: Workflow name (required)\\n      ref: e106\\n      name: The filename of the publishing workflow. This file should exist in the .github/workflows/ directory in the repository configured above.\\n      ref: e107\\n      name: Environment name (optional)\\n      ref: e108\\n      name: The name of the GitHub Actions environment that the above workflow uses for publishing. This should be configured under the repository's settings. While not required, a dedicated publishing environmen\\n      ref: e109\\n      name: Read more about GitLab CI/CD OpenID Connect support here.\\n      ref: e110\\n      name: PyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e111\\n      name: The project (on PyPI) that will be created when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e112\\n      name: Namespace (required)\\n      ref: e113\\n      name: The GitLab username or GitLab group/subgroup namespace that the project is under\\n      ref: e114\\n      name: Project name (required)\\n      ref: e115\\n      name: The name of the GitLab project that contains the publishing workflow\\n      ref: e116\\n      name: Top-level pipeline file path (required)\\n      ref: e117\\n      name: The file path of the top-level pipeline, relative to the project's root. This file should exist in the project configured above (external pipelines are not supported).\\n      ref: e118\\n      name: Environment name (optional)\\n      ref: e119\\n      name: The name of the GitLab CI/CD environment that the above workflow uses for publishing. This should be configured under the project's settings. While not required, a dedicated publishing environment is\\n      ref: e120\\n      name: Read more about Google's OpenID Connect support here.\\n      ref: e121\\n      name: PyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e122\\n      name: The project (on PyPI) that will be created when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e123\\n      name: Email (required)\\n      ref: e124\\n      name: The email address of the account or service account used to publish.\\n      ref: e125\\n      name: Subject (optional)\\n      ref: e126\\n      name: The subject is the numeric ID that represents the principal making the request. While not required, providing the subject further restricts the identity used for publishing. role: heading\\n      name: Trusted Publisher Management\\n      ref: e88\\n      level: 1\\n      name: OpenID Connect (OIDC) provides a flexible, credential-free mechanism for delegating publishing authority for a PyPI package to a trusted third party service, like GitHub Actions.\\n      ref: e89\\n      name: PyPI users and projects can use trusted publishers to automate their release processes, without needing to use API tokens or passwords.\\n      ref: e90\\n      name: You can read more about trusted publishers and how to use them here .\\n      ref: e91\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Manage publishers\\n      ref: e92\\n      level: 2\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Projects with active publishers\\n      ref: e93\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Pending publishers\\n      ref: e94\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Add a new pending publisher\\n      ref: e95\\n      level: 2\\n      name: \\\"You can use this page to register \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" trusted publishers.\\\"\\n      ref: e96\\n      name: These publishers behave similarly to trusted publishers registered against specific projects, except that they allow users to create the project if it doesn't already exist. Once the project is create\\n      ref: e97\\n      name: \\\"Configuring a \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher for a project name does not reserve that name. Until the project is created, any other user may create it, including via their own \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher.\\\"\\n      ref: e98\\n      name: Read more about GitHub Actions' OpenID Connect support here .\\n      ref: e99\\n      name: TestPyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e100\\n      name: The project that will be created on TestPyPI when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e101\\n      name: Owner (required)\\n      ref: e102\\n      name: The GitHub organization name or GitHub username that owns the repository\\n      ref: e103\\n      name: Repository name (required)\\n      ref: e104\\n      name: The name of the GitHub repository that contains the publishing workflow\\n      ref: e105\\n      name: Workflow name (required)\\n      ref: e106\\n      name: The filename of the publishing workflow. This file should exist in the .github/workflows/ directory in the repository configured above.\\n      ref: e107\\n      name: Environment name (optional)\\n      ref: e108\\n      name: The name of the GitHub Actions environment that the above workflow uses for publishing. This should be configured under the repository's settings. While not required, a dedicated publishing environmen\\n      ref: e109\\n      name: Read more about GitLab CI/CD OpenID Connect support here.\\n      ref: e110\\n      name: PyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e111\\n      name: The project (on PyPI) that will be created when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e112\\n      name: Namespace (required)\\n      ref: e113\\n      name: The GitLab username or GitLab group/subgroup namespace that the project is under\\n      ref: e114\\n      name: Project name (required)\\n      ref: e115\\n      name: The name of the GitLab project that contains the publishing workflow\\n      ref: e116\\n      name: Top-level pipeline file path (required)\\n      ref: e117\\n      name: The file path of the top-level pipeline, relative to the project's root. This file should exist in the project configured above (external pipelines are not supported).\\n      ref: e118\\n      name: Environment name (optional)\\n      ref: e119\\n      name: The name of the GitLab CI/CD environment that the above workflow uses for publishing. This should be configured under the project's settings. role: heading\\n      name: Trusted Publisher Management\\n      ref: e88\\n      level: 1\\n      name: OpenID Connect (OIDC) provides a flexible, credential-free mechanism for delegating publishing authority for a PyPI package to a trusted third party service, like GitHub Actions.\\n      ref: e89\\n      name: PyPI users and projects can use trusted publishers to automate their release processes, without needing to use API tokens or passwords.\\n      ref: e90\\n      name: You can read more about trusted publishers and how to use them here .\\n      ref: e91\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Manage publishers\\n      ref: e92\\n      level: 2\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Projects with active publishers\\n      ref: e93\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Pending publishers\\n      ref: e94\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Add a new pending publisher\\n      ref: e95\\n      level: 2\\n      name: \\\"You can use this page to register \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" trusted publishers.\\\"\\n      ref: e96\\n      name: These publishers behave similarly to trusted publishers registered against specific projects, except that they allow users to create the project if it doesn't already exist. Once the project is create\\n      ref: e97\\n      name: \\\"Configuring a \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher for a project name does not reserve that name. Until the project is created, any other user may create it, including via their own \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher.\\\"\\n      ref: e98\\n      name: Read more about GitHub Actions' OpenID Connect support here .\\n      ref: e99\\n      name: TestPyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e100\\n      name: The project that will be created on TestPyPI when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e101\\n      name: Owner (required)\\n      ref: e102\\n      name: The GitHub organization name or GitHub username that owns the repository\\n      ref: e103\\n      name: Repository name (required)\\n      ref: e104\\n      name: The name of the GitHub repository that contains the publishing workflow\\n      ref: e105\\n      name: Workflow name (required)\\n      ref: e106\\n      name: The filename of the publishing workflow. This file should exist in the .github/workflows/ directory in the repository configured above.\\n      ref: e107\\n      name: Environment name (optional)\\n      ref: e108\\n      name: The name of the GitHub Actions environment that the above workflow uses for publishing. role: heading\\n      name: Trusted Publisher Management\\n      ref: e88\\n      level: 1\\n      name: OpenID Connect (OIDC) provides a flexible, credential-free mechanism for delegating publishing authority for a PyPI package to a trusted third party service, like GitHub Actions.\\n      ref: e89\\n      name: PyPI users and projects can use trusted publishers to automate their release processes, without needing to use API tokens or passwords.\\n      ref: e90\\n      name: You can read more about trusted publishers and how to use them here .\\n      ref: e91\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Trusted Publisher Management\\n      ref: e88\\n      level: 1\\n      name: OpenID Connect (OIDC) provides a flexible, credential-free mechanism for delegating publishing authority for a PyPI package to a trusted third party service, like GitHub Actions.\\n      ref: e89\\n      name: PyPI users and projects can use trusted publishers to automate their release processes, without needing to use API tokens or passwords.\\n      ref: e90\\n      name: You can read more about trusted publishers and how to use them here .\\n      ref: e91\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Manage publishers\\n      ref: e92\\n      level: 2\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Projects with active publishers\\n      ref: e93\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Pending publishers\\n      ref: e94\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Add a new pending publisher\\n      ref: e95\\n      level: 2\\n      name: \\\"You can use this page to register \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" trusted publishers.\\\"\\n      ref: e96\\n      name: These publishers behave similarly to trusted publishers registered against specific projects, except that they allow users to create the project if it doesn't already exist. Once the project is create\\n      ref: e97\\n      name: \\\"Configuring a \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher for a project name does not reserve that name. Until the project is created, any other user may create it, including via their own \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher.\\\"\\n      ref: e98\\n      name: Read more about GitHub Actions' OpenID Connect support here .\\n      ref: e99\\n      name: TestPyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e100\\n      name: The project that will be created on TestPyPI when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e101\\n      name: Owner (required)\\n      ref: e102\\n      name: The GitHub organization name or GitHub username that owns the repository\\n      ref: e103\\n      name: Repository name (required)\\n      ref: e104\\n      name: The name of the GitHub repository that contains the publishing workflow\\n      ref: e105\\n      name: Workflow name (required)\\n      ref: e106\\n      name: The filename of the publishing workflow. This file should exist in the .github/workflows/ directory in the repository configured above.\\n      ref: e107\\n      name: Environment name (optional)\\n      ref: e108\\n      name: The name of the GitHub Actions environment that the above workflow uses for publishing. <tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="mcp-cursor-ide-browser-cursor-ide-browser-browser_snapshot"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **mcp-cursor-ide-browser-cursor-ide-browser-browser_snapshot**</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet)**_

I notice the button is shown as a button with `role: button` in the snapshot but I keep getting "stale element" errors. Let me try a simpler approach - click directly on the button using a direct action immediately after the snapshot without any delay. I'll use the current e26 ref that was just returned.

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="mcp-cursor-ide-browser-cursor-ide-browser-browser_click">
<details>
<summary>Tool use: **mcp-cursor-ide-browser-cursor-ide-browser-browser_click**</summary>


Parameters:

```json
{
  "tools": [
    {
      "name": "browser_click",
      "parameters": "{\"element\":\"Add button\",\"ref\":\"e26\",\"viewId\":\"07bc7207-742c-4857-bd09-55960c120a8c\",\"take_screenshot_afterwards\":true,\"headless\":true}",
      "serverName": "cursor-ide-browser"
    }
  ]
}
```
Additional data:

```json
{
  "status": "success"
}
```
Result:

```json
{
  "result": "{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"Error: Stale element reference: e26 expected a button but found  (input). The page may have changed. Take a new snapshot.\\n\\nRecovery action: browser_snapshot to see current element positions\"}]}"
}
```
Status: **completed**


</details>
</tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="mcp-cursor-ide-browser-cursor-ide-browser-browser_click"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **mcp-cursor-ide-browser-cursor-ide-browser-browser_click**</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet)**_

The error message is revealing: "e26 expected a button but found  (input)". This means the element type is changing between when I get the snapshot and when I try to click. This suggests there's dynamic JavaScript modifying the form. 

Let me try a different approach - instead of clicking the button, let me try using the browser's form submission by pressing Enter while focused on one of the form fields. The focus is currently on e20 (TestPyPI Project Name field) according to the snapshot.

_**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="mcp-cursor-ide-browser-cursor-ide-browser-browser_press_key">

      "serverName": "cursor-ide-browser"
    }
  ]
}
```

}

}

role: heading\\n      name: Trusted Publisher Management\\n      ref: e88\\n      level: 1\\n      name: OpenID Connect (OIDC) provides a flexible, credential-free mechanism for delegating publishing authority for a PyPI package to a trusted third party service, like GitHub Actions.\\n      ref: e89\\n      name: PyPI users and projects can use trusted publishers to automate their release processes, without needing to use API tokens or passwords.\\n      ref: e90\\n      name: You can read more about trusted publishers and how to use them here .\\n      ref: e91\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Manage publishers\\n      ref: e92\\n      level: 2\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Projects with active publishers\\n      ref: e93\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Pending publishers\\n      ref: e94\\n      level: 3\\n    - role: heading\\n      name: Add a new pending publisher\\n      ref: e95\\n      level: 2\\n      name: \\\"You can use this page to register \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" trusted publishers.\\\"\\n      ref: e96\\n      name: These publishers behave similarly to trusted publishers registered against specific projects, except that they allow users to create the project if it doesn't already exist. Once the project is create\\n      ref: e97\\n      name: \\\"Configuring a \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher for a project name does not reserve that name. Until the project is created, any other user may create it, including via their own \\\\\\\"pending\\\\\\\" publisher.\\\"\\n      ref: e98\\n      name: Read more about GitHub Actions' OpenID Connect support here .\\n      ref: e99\\n      name: TestPyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e100\\n      name: The project that will be created on TestPyPI when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e101\\n      name: Owner (required)\\n      ref: e102\\n      name: The GitHub organization name or GitHub username that owns the repository\\n      ref: e103\\n      name: Repository name (required)\\n      ref: e104\\n      name: The name of the GitHub repository that contains the publishing workflow\\n      ref: e105\\n      name: Workflow name (required)\\n      ref: e106\\n      name: The filename of the publishing workflow. This file should exist in the .github/workflows/ directory in the repository configured above.\\n      ref: e107\\n      name: Environment name (optional)\\n      ref: e108\\n      name: The name of the GitHub Actions environment that the above workflow uses for publishing. This should be configured under the repository's settings. While not required, a dedicated publishing environmen\\n      ref: e109\\n      name: Read more about GitLab CI/CD OpenID Connect support here.\\n      ref: e110\\n      name: PyPI Project Name (required)\\n      ref: e111\\n      name: The project (on PyPI) that will be created when this publisher is used\\n      ref: e112\\n      name: Namespace (required)\\n      ref: e113\\n      name: The GitLab username or GitLab group/subgroup namespace that the project is under\\n      ref: e114\\n      name: Project name (required)\\n      ref: e115\\n      name: The name of the GitLab project that contains the publishing workflow\\n      ref: e116\\n      name: Top-level pipeline file path (required)\\n      ref: e117\\n      name: The file path of the top-level pipeline, relative to the project's root. This file should exist in the project configured above (external pipelines are not supported).\\n      ref: e118\\n      name: Environment name (optional)\\n      ref: e119\\n      name: The name of the GitLab CI/CD environment that the above workflow uses for publishing. _**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet)**_

I'm encountering a technical issue with the browser automation. The "Add" button is showing as "readonly" and is also dynamically changing its element type between snapshots (from button to input), which is causing stale element errors.

The automation pattern you provided (fill form, click Add, press Enter) isn't working due to these technical limitations. The browser tools are unable to interact with this specific form element.

Would you like me to:
1. Try adding these publishers manually (you could take over control)
2. Continue trying different automation approaches
3. Report this as a limitation and move on

Given the complexity and the stale element issues, it appears this specific form has JavaScript that's incompatible with the current browser automation tools.
